metadata.title: Consumer Theory I: Utility & Marshallian Demand
learning_objectives: Analyze special cases: Cobb-Douglas, perfect substitutes/complements

questions.question: Sketch indifference curves for: (a) U = x₁⁰·⁵x₂⁰·⁵, (b) U = 2x₁ + 3x₂, (c) U = min{x₁, 2x₂}, (d) U = x₁ + ln(x₂)
For U = x₁⁰·³x₂⁰·⁷, find MRS at (10, 20). Interpret economically.
Show that U = x₁x₂ and V = ln(x₁) + ln(x₂) represent identical preferences.
Budget: p₁=4, p₂=5, m=100. (a) Write budget equation. (b) Find intercepts. (c) If p₁ rises to 8, describe change.
U = x₁⁰·⁴x₂⁰·⁶, prices p₁=2, p₂=3, income m=120. Find optimal bundle, utility, and λ.
U = 3x₁ + 4x₂, p₁=6, p₂=10, m=60. Find optimal bundle.
hints: Buy the good with higher MU per dollar

questions.question: U = min{2x₁, x₂}, p₁=4, p₂=2, m=40. Find optimal bundle.
U = x₁ + 4√x₂, p₁=1, p₂=4, m=20. Find optimal bundle and verify zero income effect for x₂.
For Cobb-Douglas U = x₁⁰·⁵x₂⁰·⁵ with m=100: (a) Find demand when p₁=2, p₂=2. (b) Find demand when p₁=4, p₂=2. (c) Calculate price elasticity of x₁.
Write R function to solve Cobb-Douglas utility max numerically using nloptr. Compare with analytical solution.
Household allocates food budget m=200 between staples (x₁) and meat (x₂). U = x₁⁰·⁷x₂⁰·³, p₁=2, p₂=10. (a) Find demands. (b) Government subsidizes meat: p₂→5. New demands? (c) Income elasticities?
U = ln(x₁) + 2ln(x₂), p₁=1, p₂=2, m=12. Solve and verify SOC using bordered Hessian.
hints: For max with one constraint: det(H̄) > 0
